<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: planning-as-a-control-layer
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/planning-as-a-control-layer.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/planning-as-a-control-layer.qmd@d028c2c0c
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Planning as a Control Layer: DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage

A command can be valid and still be unready to act on. It may have a clear
objective, explicit constraints, a known output shape, and a verification rule,
but still lack the shape of the work. Something has to decide what steps exist,
which steps depend on other steps, what context is missing, which tools would
be needed, where authority stops, and what would count as a reason to refuse,
pause, or replan.

That layer is planning. Once the plan exists, it also has to become
schedulable. Some nodes need expensive cognition, some need a small specialist,
some need a tool, some need human review, and some should remain blocked. The
point is not to use the cheapest route. The point is to spend the right kind of
intelligence only where the node's risk, context, quality predicate, and
verification burden justify it.

In this stack, planning is not brainstorming. It is not a private chain of
thought, and it is not a promise that the system knows how to execute the work.
Planning is a controlled delay before action. It turns an accepted command into
a visible plan graph so the system can inspect the work before the work touches
memory, tools, users, money, files, networks, or external systems.

Planning adds one distinct idea to the book's repeated record-and-gate pattern:
ordered obligation. The plan says which obligations exist, which are blocked,
which are dispatchable, which need stronger evidence, and what changed when
the graph was replanned. That is why planning is not just another place to
write a checklist; it is the layer that prevents accepted intent from becoming
authorized work too early.

The planner earns its place when it makes non-action legible. A good plan does
not only say what should happen next. It says which nodes are blocked, which
dependencies are missing, which verifier is unavailable, which source is not
adequate, which tool exceeds authority, which budget cannot support the quality
predicate, and which stop condition has already fired. Those are not side
notes. They are the point of the control layer.

Planning is useful because many dangerous actions begin as plausible steps. A
graph full of plausible steps is not a governed system. The graph becomes
governed only when proposed work, blocked work, reviewable work, dispatchable
work, executed work, replanned work, and stopped work remain different states.

If the command contract answers "what is this system allowed to try," the plan
graph answers "which parts of that attempt are real work, which parts are only
hypotheses, and which parts must stay blocked." That distinction is what makes
planning a control layer instead of a prelude to tool use.

## Problem

Goals need to become governed plans with dependencies, budgets, risk limits,
tool choices, and stopping conditions.

The previous chapters built the front of the stack. Human intent becomes a
formal input. Intent-to-execution contracts preserve the difference between
what a person wants and what an executable system is allowed to do. Command
contracts turn rich language into fields that can be inspected and refused.

But a command contract is still not a work schedule. It does not tell the
system how the task decomposes, what order the pieces should happen in, where
context must be fetched, which worker or specialist is appropriate, what budget
is sufficient, how failure changes the route, or when the safest result is to
stop.

Without a planning layer, those decisions get pushed into places that should
not own them. Memory starts to create work by deciding what context implies.
Reasoning starts to spend authority by turning an argument into an action.
Execution starts to discover dependencies after side effects have already
happened. Routing starts to treat cost and capability as the whole problem.

Planning gives the stack a place to say: here is the work we think exists, here
is why it is ordered this way, here is what is missing, here is what cannot be
done yet, here is the route each node deserves, and here is the narrow subset
that may become typed jobs.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Modern agent systems already contain planning ideas. ReAct keeps action and
observation in a loop. Tree of Thoughts explores intermediate paths. PDDL and
SHOP2 separate domains, problems, and hierarchical task decomposition. Task and
motion planning separates discrete plans from feasibility. Behavior trees and
GOAP-style systems show practical ways to organize reactive action. AutoGen and
similar multi-agent systems make orchestration explicit. TLA+ gives a language
for system modeling and state transitions.

Those references matter because they show that planning is not a new word for
"thinking harder." Planning has long meant structure: states, operators,
preconditions, decomposition, feasibility, observation, coordination, and
modeling.

The ASI Stack uses that background differently. It does not claim to reproduce
those systems, beat their benchmarks, or provide a deployed planner. The claim
here is architectural: planning should be its own control layer between
accepted commands and execution.

That separation matters because the planner has a different failure mode than
memory, reasoning, routing, or execution.

Memory can over-expose context. Reasoning can rationalize unsupported steps.
Routing can pick the cheapest inadequate worker. Execution can create real
effects before the obligation is understood. Planning can fail by making the
work look orderly while hiding whether it is authorized, verified, affordable,
or even executable.

The most dangerous planner is not the one that admits it is blocked. The
dangerous planner is the one that turns every plausible node into a job.

## Core Claim

Planning should be a separate control layer that turns accepted command
contracts into schedulable DAGs with explicit dependencies, authority ceilings,
context demands, capability-tier assignments, adequacy contracts, verification
burdens, cost/quality ledgers, escalation routes, and residuals (evidence
boundary: architectural argument).

PlanForge supplies the planning-middleware vocabulary: decomposition,
dependencies, scheduling, intelligence tiering, fallback, and replanning. Its
compiler-architecture variant sharpens the idea that orchestration should be
compiled into task graphs rather than improvised through serial chat. VIEA
supplies the accepted-command-to-artifact spine. Cognitive Compilation supplies
the compiler-pipeline pressure: goals become source plans, semantic
intermediate representations, validators, and repairable artifacts. Software
Magic Grimoire supplies bounded workflow language: handoffs, guards, entry and
exit rules, loops, recursion, and recovery paths. TokenMana keeps budgets from
collapsing into token counts by including latency, load, review capacity, burst
limits, and human friction. Coherence Exchange remains speculative
intelligence-arbitrage context. MoECOT supplies implementation-reference
context for orchestration, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, residuals, and
promotion blockers.

Those sources support the architecture vocabulary. They do not prove planner
quality in this repository. They do not show a deployed scheduler, dependency
soundness, context-demand prediction, selected-tier adequacy, cost savings,
runtime replanning, or end-to-end tool execution.

## Mechanism

The planning artifact is the plan graph.

A plan graph receives a validated command contract and expands it into
assumptions, nodes, dependencies, context requests, tool requirements,
authority requirements, budgets, verification plans, replanning policy, stop
conditions, dispatch state, residuals, and non-claims.

The graph is not a job queue. It is a control surface. It lets the system ask
which parts of the work are merely proposed, which are blocked, which need
review, which are ready to dispatch, which have already been dispatched, which
changed after feedback, and which have stopped.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Accepted command contract"] --> B["Plan graph"]
  B --> C["Assumptions, dependencies, and node states"]
  B --> D["Context, authority, risk, and budget requests"]
  B --> E["Capability tier and adequacy contract"]
  E --> F["Quality predicate and verifier"]
  C --> G{"Dispatch gate"}
  D --> G
  F --> G
  G -- "proposed, blocked, or failed" --> H["Residual, review, escalation, or replan"]
  G -- "dispatchable" --> I["Dispatch receipt"]
  I --> J["Semantic IR or typed job"]
  J --> K["Runtime feedback"]
  K --> L{"Replan needed?"}
  L -- "yes" --> B
  L -- "no" --> M["Plan ledger and cost-quality ledger"]
  H --> M
```

The key boundary is the dispatch receipt. A node can be useful without being
dispatchable. It can clarify a dependency, expose a missing source, estimate a
budget, or identify an approval requirement. But it should not become a typed
job until the graph records satisfied constraints, context references, an
authority basis, a verification plan, and a parent command.

That dispatch receipt is the bridge to the Labor OS. Without it, a downstream
tool call may still happen in an ad hoc system, but the book should not treat
that as governed execution.

### Node States

A plan graph needs more than "todo" and "done."

Proposed nodes are decomposition candidates. They may be useful, but they have
not earned dispatch. Nodes blocked on context need missing material or a
narrower target. Nodes blocked on authority need a new contract, an approval
route, or a refusal. Nodes blocked on dependencies wait for upstream artifacts,
reviews, or decisions. Nodes blocked on verification need a success predicate
before the system can honestly lower them into work.

Dispatchable nodes are different. They have satisfied constraints, context,
authority, dependencies, and verification plans. Dispatched nodes have produced
job packets. Replanned nodes record what changed after feedback while
preserving the original authority and stop-condition boundaries. Stopped nodes
preserve the reason work ended: denial, risk, budget exhaustion, missing
source, failed predicate, or triggered stop condition.

This state vocabulary turns the plan into a governed object. The graph can
contain ideas, questions, dependencies, and refusals without treating them all
as permission.

### DAG Scheduling And Intelligence Arbitrage

PlanForge sharpens the plan graph into a schedulable DAG. A DAG gives the
system a way to split work, expose dependency order, parallelize where safe,
and keep local repair local. If one source-reading node used stale evidence,
the scheduler should identify the affected subgraph, repair that node, rerun
only the dependents whose assumptions changed, and preserve unaffected work.

This is also where intelligence arbitrage becomes governable. Arbitrage is not
the slogan "use a cheaper model." It is the discipline of matching each node to
the minimum adequate capability plus enough verification to know that the route
was adequate. A cheap route is acceptable only when it has a bounded work
contract, an adequacy contract, a verifier, and a fallback. A stronger model,
specialist core, tool, human reviewer, or tribunal route is justified when
risk, source uncertainty, context demand, quality predicate, or failure cost
makes the cheaper route inadequate.

Every node therefore carries two contracts. The work contract names what the
node may do. The adequacy contract names what would make the result usable by
downstream dependents. Scheduling without the adequacy contract creates output
without reliable progress.

The reader should notice the reversal: planning does not earn trust by finding
more actions. It earns trust by finding the smallest set of actions that are
authorized, adequate, ordered, and worth dispatching.

A cost-quality ledger keeps the economics honest. It should count tokens,
latency, tool cost, review cost, repair cost, failed attempts, human cleanup,
and residuals before calling a route efficient. Moving verification or cleanup
outside the ledger is not intelligence arbitrage. It is displaced cost.

## Interfaces

Planning sits between command contracts and execution, but it touches almost
every layer around it.

Alignment filters which goals may become planning inputs. Governance constrains
authority, review, refusal, appeal, and stop conditions. VCM supplies context
packets and source adequacy. Routing selects specialists only after the plan
names requirements, adequacy contracts, route assignments, and escalation
paths. Reasoning and verification help judge assumptions and success
predicates. Cognitive Compilation lowers accepted plan obligations into
semantic IR. Execution receives typed jobs only from dispatchable plan nodes.
Evidence records assumptions, residuals, cost-quality decisions, verification
results, replanning events, and non-claims.

Minimum plan-graph fields include:

- `plan_id`
- `command_contract`
- `assumptions`
- `nodes`
- `dependencies`
- `context_requests`
- `tool_requirements`
- `authority_requirements`
- `authority_budget`
- `risk_budget`
- `compute_budget`
- `verification_plan`
- `replanning_policy`
- `replanning_history`
- `stop_conditions`
- `node_lifecycle_states`
- `blocked_nodes`
- `dispatch_receipts`
- `residual_register`
- `dispatch_state`
- `capability_tiers`
- `adequacy_contracts`
- `quality_predicates`
- `route_assignments`
- `merge_conditions`
- `cost_quality_ledger`
- `non_claims`

Those fields do not make the planner good. They make the planner inspectable.
The difference matters. A record shape can be validated before anyone knows
whether the decomposition was clever, efficient, or complete.

## Invariants

The planning layer has a small set of obligations.

Plans expose constraints and stop conditions. Runtime replanning preserves
authority limits. Tool selection is justified by task requirements. Only
dispatchable nodes lower into typed jobs. Candidate routes, blocked nodes, and
review notes are not executable permission. Dependency order is respected
before dispatch. Cost savings do not remove required verification. Failed
quality predicates route to escalation or residuals. Parallel branches merge
only when their assumptions, source versions, authority ceilings, and quality
predicates are compatible.

The cleanest invariant is authority inheritance. A plan node should not exceed
the parent command's authority ceiling unless governance explicitly narrows or
changes the boundary. Replanning may change order, route, or decomposition. It
may not silently widen authority by calling the change adaptation.

Stop conditions need the same treatment. A failed path can produce a better
route, but it should not erase the condition that told the original work when
to stop, escalate, or ask for review.

The plan ledger should also preserve rejected or discarded routes when those
routes explain risk, budget, source inadequacy, or verification burden. A plan
that only records the happy path is hard to audit after failure.

The efficiency invariant is bounded by verification. A low-cost route counts
only if the node's quality predicate, fallback, downstream utility, and
residual accounting remain attached. If the system hides the repair cost, it
has not saved intelligence; it has hidden the bill.

## Failure Modes

Planning fails when the graph creates the appearance of control without
actually controlling dispatch.

Scope creep appears when the plan adds goals that were never accepted. Planning
without replanning appears when the first failure produces improvisation
instead of controlled repair. Tool overreach appears when a useful step asks
for a tool, permission, or budget beyond the parent contract. Dispatch
laundering appears when a proposed or blocked node becomes a job because it was
written down in the graph. Replanning erasure appears when feedback changes the
plan while hiding the authority delta, stop-condition delta, or residual.
Wrong tier selection appears when a cheap route produces plausible but
inadequate work. Dependency cycles make the graph look planned while no valid
dispatch order exists. Merge laundering appears when parallel branches pass
locally while relying on conflicting assumptions or source versions.
Arbitrage laundering appears when the scheduler reports lower cost while
moving verification, repair, or human cleanup outside the ledger.

These are not rare corner cases. They are the default failure pressures of
agentic systems. Once a system has momentum, a plausible plan can become an
excuse for action. The control layer exists to interrupt that slide.

The best failure signal is often a visible blocked node. A blocked context node
says the source is not adequate. A blocked authority node says the system lacks
permission. A blocked verification node says success cannot yet be judged. A
stopped node says the right outcome was non-execution.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a public plan-graph record and a small
cross-record gate.

The repository already has the basic pieces for that narrow slice: a
`plan_graph` schema, a PlanForge DAG schema, typed-job records, semantic-atom
records, and a synthetic plan-execution contract harness. The harness checks
that command contracts, plan graphs, DAGs, semantic atoms, and typed jobs agree
on parentage and obligation boundaries. It rejects cycles, contract mismatch,
dispatch without receipt, lost requirements, and approval bypasses. It also
accepts a blocked-authority plan that preserves residuals without pretending
to dispatch work.

That is useful, but small. It validates cross-record discipline, not planner
intelligence. It does not measure decomposition quality, context-demand
prediction, scheduler performance, runtime replanning, or tool execution.

The minimum next improvement is a tiny scheduler-state fixture with one ready
node, one node blocked by missing context, one cheap route rejected because its
adequacy predicate fails, one branch merge blocked by conflicting assumptions,
and one local repair that touches only the affected subgraph. That fixture
demonstrates the semantics of planning as control and DAG scheduling without
implying that the system can plan well in the open world.

The planning scheduler-state probe now implements that next slice. It uses
two synthetic scheduler traces: one with a ready dispatchable node, a
context-blocked node, a failed cheap route, and a blocked merge; and one local
repair trace that touches only the affected subgraph. It also rejects seven bad
paths, including dispatching blocked work, treating missing context as ready,
accepting a failed cheap route, merging conflicting assumptions, erasing
authority during replanning, accepting a dependency cycle, and hiding repair
or residual costs.

That makes the control surface easier to inspect. It is a trace-shape check,
not a deployed scheduler result. It does not prove planner quality,
context-demand prediction, route quality, selected-tier adequacy, or runtime
replanning.

The runtime-replan delta audit adds one more small guard. It asks what must be
true when feedback changes a plan after work has started. The audit uses two synthetic runtime-replan traces. In the synthetic fixture, a source repair can
rerun only the affected subgraph, while an
authority-blocked replan must stop as a residual instead of producing a
dispatch receipt. The bad cases are the ones a real system would be tempted to
hide: widened authority, erased stop conditions, unnecessary reruns, missing
context or verification deltas, missing residual ownership, and a quiet claim
that the chapter is now proven.

That audit makes the record shape sharper. It is not a deployed planner result,
not a live feedback loop, and not evidence that the scheduler chooses
good routes. It says only that a runtime replan record must preserve the same
control boundaries the chapter argues for in prose. The no-promotion record
for this audit treats it as a blocking decision: it names what would be needed
for stronger Planning evidence, but it does not move the chapter support state.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a live control plane for work.

In that mature system, planning would receive accepted command contracts and
maintain plan graphs over time. Strategic, tactical, and runtime views would
share the same parent contract. Every node would carry assumptions,
dependencies, context needs, authority basis, budgets, risk limits,
verification plans, stop conditions, capability tier, adequacy contract, route
assignment, merge condition, escalation path, and residual ownership. Dispatch
receipts would bind each job to a plan node, a satisfied-constraint set, a
context packet, a verifier, and the parent command.

The mature system would be useful even when nothing executes. It would show
blocked context, blocked authority, blocked verification, cyclic dependency,
budget pressure, source inadequacy, missing approval, and human-review queues
before side effects occur. It would let a team inspect why work is waiting,
why work was stopped, and why some possible route was not allowed.

This endpoint is still an architectural target. The current repository does
not contain a deployed planning control plane. It contains a schema, source
mapping, finite-record proof hooks, and synthetic cross-record tests that make
the boundary more explicit.

At full product depth, the planner is also a governed capacity allocator for
cognitive work. It can show why one node used a small local model, another
used a specialist core, another required human approval, and another stayed
blocked. It reports efficiency only after it counts verification, repair,
merge risk, review, fallback, failed attempts, and residual cost.

## What Is Proven, And What Is Not

The Lean module `AsiStackProofs.Planning` models finite records around
authority inheritance, dispatchability, blocked plans, replanning, residuals,
and non-claims. It proves small properties over those records: unsatisfied
constraints block dispatch, valid dispatchable records have required gates,
blocked records have no dispatch receipt, replanned records preserve control
residuals, and valid planning records keep non-claims present.

The Lean module `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` adds finite-record predicates for
DAG scheduling and failed-quality fallback. It checks that a dispatchable
finite plan graph carries an acyclicity certificate and ordered dependency
edges, and that a failed quality predicate cannot count as a valid node
outcome unless it escalates or emits a residual.

The newer runtime-replan proof bridge stays in the same narrow lane. It models
recorded deltas, not deployed behavior. It rejects authority widening,
stop-condition erasure, unscoped repair, missing context or verification
deltas, blocked-authority dispatch, support promotion, and missing non-claim
boundaries before a modeled runtime delta can be accepted.

Those theorems are useful because they make the intended boundary executable
as a formal envelope. They are not broad correctness proofs. They do not prove
that a real planner decomposes tasks well, finds the right dependencies,
predicts missing context, chooses the best route, preserves authority in a
live runtime, handles real feedback, measures cost savings, proves
selected-tier adequacy, or succeeds at execution.

The honest status is therefore: planning support remains `argument`. The
formal and synthetic artifacts make the shape of the claim more disciplined,
but they do not promote the chapter core claim.

## Summary

Planning is the stack's control surface between accepted commands and typed
jobs. It converts intent into a visible graph of obligations: assumptions,
dependencies, context, tools, authority, budgets, capability tiers, adequacy
contracts, verification, stop conditions, residuals, and dispatch state.

The chapter's central distinction is proposed work versus authorized work. A
plan may contain many useful nodes, but only dispatchable nodes should lower
into jobs. Blocked nodes, missing sources, authority gaps, failed predicates,
and stop conditions are valuable planning outputs because they prevent
plausible action from becoming unauthorized action.

That is why planning belongs between command contracts and execution. It lets
the system slow down in a structured way. It turns "we could do this" into the
more important question: "which parts of this are actually allowed to become
work, and what kind of intelligence would make each part adequate?"

The next chapter takes those dispatchable obligations and asks for their build
language. A plan can say what should happen; cognitive compilation says what
must be preserved while the artifact is being made.

## Handoff

Planning has treated the plan as a controlled, schedulable object. The next
chapter, Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR, asks what form scheduled work
must take before it can become artifacts. It turns plan obligations into
semantic atoms, validators, target constraints, lowering receipts, and repair
ledgers so the stack can audit what survived the translation from plan to
artifact.
